title: New quantum information perspectives in the axion--photon and neutrino systems
authors: Aaditya Datar, Arun M. Thalapillil, Palak Thareja
abstract: In this work, we broach a quantum information-theoretic treatment of axion--photon mixing. Motivated by an emerging class of quantum-enhanced axion searches, we analyse the two-level single-excitation sector of axion--photon oscillations, demonstrating how the coupled dynamics naturally generate bipartite axion--photon mode entanglement. We study in detail the ensuing aspects of entanglement entropy, concurrence, negativity, quantum mutual information and discord, and capacity of entanglement, and the corresponding neutrino analogues wherever novel and previously unaddressed. In particular, we highlight the characteristic features that connect maximal axion--photon entanglement to resonant or strong-mixing conversion, and the distinct thresholds for the extremal values attained by the quantum information measures. We study aspects of the Mandelstam--Tamm and Margolus--Levitin quantum speed limits for both the axion--photon and neutrino systems. While orthogonalisation occurs only at axion--photon resonance, or at maximal neutrino mixing, where the two bounds coincide, away from these limits, the Margolus--Levitin bound is saturated at maximal conversion, while the Mandelstam--Tamm bound is generally weaker. We also study an entanglement quantum speed limit for axion--photon conversion, that separates into detuning-dominated and magnetic-mixing-dominated regimes, and find that it is saturated for a period and then the bound becomes weak. The results in this work identify the quantum resources and limiting timescales intrinsic to axion--photon conversion, and connect axion phenomenology, neutrino oscillations and quantum information theory.
summary: This work applies quantum information theory to study axion-photon mixing, treating it as a quantum oscillation problem analogous to neutrino oscillations. By analyzing entanglement measures and deriving an entanglement quantum speed limit, the authors establish fundamental, quantifiable limits on the rate and nature of the conversion process. This connects particle phenomenology directly to the fundamental principles of quantum information theory.
main_problem: The paper develops a quantum information-theoretic framework to analyze axion-photon mixing, drawing parallels with neutrino oscillations.
main_result: It identifies characteristic thresholds for quantum information measures and derives an entanglement quantum speed limit that characterizes the intrinsic timescales of the conversion process.
method: The analysis employs various quantum information measures (e.g., entanglement entropy, concurrence, discord) and compares multiple quantum speed limits (Mandelstam-Tamm, Margolus-Levitin, and entanglement-specific bounds).
model_or_system: The system is the axion-photon pair coupled in the presence of a transverse external magnetic field, analyzed in the single-excitation sector. The dynamics are compared to the two-flavor neutrino oscillation model.
key_observables: Entanglement entropy, concurrence, negativity, quantum mutual information, discord, capacity of entanglement, and various quantum speed limits.
important_parameters: Axion mass ($\alpha$), mixing parameter ($ eta$), detuning, and the external magnetic field strength ($B_{	ext{ext}}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on the single-excitation sector, and comparisons between bases (interaction vs. propagation) highlight structural differences in entanglement measures.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of entanglement measures and the entanglement quantum speed limit on the mixing parameters ($\alpha$ and $ eta$), showing distinct saturation and functional behaviors in different parameter regimes.
paper_structure: The paper systematically reviews axion-photon mixing, investigates various quantum information measures, and concludes with a detailed study and comparison of multiple quantum speed limits applicable to the system.
why_interesting: The rigorous application of quantum information theory tools, such as entanglement speed limits, to particle physics phenomena like axion-photon conversion provides a novel, fundamental constraint on particle physics models.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Representative plot of the transition probability Pγ→a(z), for a fixed z = 6 × 1019 eV−1, merely to illustrate its dependence on the physical parameters α and β. Realistic values of the physical parameters α and β lead to very small transition probabilities.

Figure 2: An illustrative plot of the von Neumann entropy for the photon subsystem in the interaction and propagation basis, plotted as a function of the system parameters α and β. The interaction basis entropy is calculated at a fixed z = 6 × 1019 eV−1 (as for the transition probability in Fig. 1). Notice how the z-dependence in the interaction basis changes the behaviour of the entropy in comparison to the static behaviour present in the propagation basis, we observe that it is modulated by the sinusoidal behaviour characteristic of the transition probability Pγ→a(z).

Figure 3: Quantum information measures calculated for the axion–photon system, plotted as func- tions of the transition probability Pγ→a(z). Notice that all measures, except the capacity of entan- glement (which measures the fluctuation rather than the magnitude of the entanglement spectrum), follow a similar evolution—the measures, although providing complementary perspectives, are related to each other as they involve the same Schmidt weight in different functional forms. It is also impor- tant to emphasise again that the maximum transition probability attained depends on the values of α and β, and does not necessarily always span the full range [0, 1].
Figure 4: An illustrative figure depicting the entanglement QSL T EE QSL in the (α, β) parameter space for the axion–photon system.

Figure 5: The entanglement QSL time T EE QSL plotted as a function of the evolution time T, over the respective full oscillation time periods for different values of β. In the plots α = 2 × 10−19 eV (top) and α = 9 × 10−19 eV (bottom), corresponding to the regimes α < β and α > β, respectively. The vertical lines correspond to the respective oscillation half-periods, where the transition probabilities are at their maxima. The diagonal, dashed line denotes a reference line T EE QSL = T. One observes that the entanglement bound is attained and saturated till some T, making the bound tight, and beyond that point the bound is weak with T > T EE QSL.
